mission.title: Install and Configure Keda on Kubernetes
mission.description: KEDA is a Kubernetes-based Event Driven Autoscaling component that enables fine-grained autoscaling for event-driven workloads in Kubernetes. Installing KEDA allows your applications to scale based on external events, improving resource utilization and responsiveness.

steps.title: Add the KEDA Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official KEDA Helm repository to your local Helm setup with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add kedacore https://kedacore.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install KEDA using Helm
steps.description:
Install KEDA in your Kubernetes cluster with the following command, creating a namespace called 'keda':
```bash
helm install keda kedacore/keda --namespace keda --create-namespace --version 2.19.0
```

steps.title: Verify KEDA installation
steps.description:
Check that KEDA is running correctly by listing the pods in the 'keda' namespace:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace keda
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of KEDA will show the KEDA operator pod running in the 'keda' namespace. You can also verify that the resource limits are correctly applied by checking the deployment.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kedacore https://kedacore.github.io/charts
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    cpu: 500m
    memory: 512Mi
  requests:
    cpu: 250m
    memory: 256Mi

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the KEDA Helm release from your Kubernetes cluster using the following command. This will remove the deployment and associated resources from the 'keda' namespace:
```bash
helm uninstall keda --namespace keda
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were installed with KEDA. Use the following command to delete the CRDs:
```bash
kubectl delete crd scaledobjects.keda.sh
kubectl delete crd triggerauthentications.keda.sh
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'keda' namespace to clean up any remaining resources. Verify that the namespace has been removed:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace keda
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current state of KEDA by exporting the existing configuration. Use the following command to get the current deployment:
```bash
kubectl get deployment keda-operator --namespace keda -o yaml > keda-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the KEDA Helm repository and run the upgrade command to upgrade KEDA to the latest version:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade keda kedacore/keda --namespace keda --version 2.20.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the KEDA upgrade was successful by listing the pods in the 'keda' namespace and ensuring they are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods --namespace keda
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If KEDA pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for the pod to diagnose the issue:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> --namespace keda
```
Look for any error messages that may indicate configuration issues or resource constraints.

troubleshooting.title: KEDA not scaling as expected
troubleshooting.description:
If KEDA is not scaling your workloads as expected, verify that the ScaledObject configurations are correct. Check the status of your ScaledObjects:
```bash
kubectl get scaledobjects --namespace keda
```
Ensure that the metrics and triggers are properly defined.

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permissions issues
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter RBAC permission errors, ensure that the RBAC configuration has been applied correctly. Check the roles and bindings:
```bash
kubectl get roles,rolebindings --namespace keda
```
If necessary, reapply the RBAC configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Helm release not found
troubleshooting.description:
If you receive an error stating that the Helm release is not found, verify that you are using the correct namespace and release name. List all Helm releases in the namespace:
```bash
helm list --namespace keda
```

prerequisites.description: You need a running Kubernetes cluster and the Helm and kubectl command-line tools installed.
